title: Communicate results | CanadaBuys

structure_blocks.heading: Communicate results
structure_blocks.text: You must communicate the results of the evaluation of offers received as part of the solicitation process. Results must be communicated to the client, the successful offeror and the unsuccessful offerors.

structure_blocks.heading: Successful offeror(s)
structure_blocks.text: After the evaluation process is complete, notify the successful offeror(s) as soon as possible. The notification should clearly state:

structure_blocks.heading: Successful offeror(s)
structure_blocks.text: the terms of the contract award, including key conditions and next steps

structure_blocks.heading: Successful offeror(s)
structure_blocks.text: any additional documents required to finalize the contract

structure_blocks.heading: Successful offeror(s)
structure_blocks.text: Ensure that the successful offeror understands their obligations and the expected timelines for contract performance. Clear communication at this stage helps avoid misunderstandings and sets the foundation for effective contract management.

structure_blocks.heading: Successful offeror(s)
structure_blocks.text: You should also inform the client of the successful offer and provide details to ensure the smooth implementation of the contract.

structure_blocks.heading: Unsuccessful offerors
structure_blocks.text: You should notify unsuccessful offerors as soon as possible after contract award or the issuance of a standing offer or supply arrangement. It is essential to communicate this information promptly to maintain a transparent and fair procurement process.

structure_blocks.heading: Unsuccessful offerors
structure_blocks.text: Sample letters are provided as templates for structuring the notification to unsuccessful offerors in a respectful and professional manner.

structure_blocks.heading: Unsuccessful offerors
structure_blocks.text: Refer to available samples of regret letters .

structure_blocks.heading: Recourse
structure_blocks.text: Upon request, you should provide general information to offerors about the avenues that may be available for recourse, including judicial and quasi-judicial bodies to which offer protests can be made, such as the following:

structure_blocks.heading: Recourse
structure_blocks.text: Canadian International Trade Tribunal (CITT): Whether or not the CITT has jurisdiction to conduct an inquiry regarding any particular procurement will depend on factors such as the government institution conducting the procurement, the estimated value of the procurement, and the nature of the goods, services, or construction services being procured. More information can be obtained by visiting Canadian International Trade Tribunal .

structure_blocks.heading: Recourse
structure_blocks.text: Office of the Procurement Ombudsman (OPO): If the value of the procurement is below the monetary thresholds established under the Canadian Free Trade Agreement (CFTA) the OPO may have jurisdiction to conduct a review. More information can be obtained by visiting The Office of the Procurement Ombud .

structure_blocks.heading: Recourse
structure_blocks.text: An action in a Provincial/Territorial Superior Court or in Federal Court.

structure_blocks.heading: Recourse
structure_blocks.text: There are strict deadlines for bringing offer protests, and the time periods vary depending on where the offer protest is brought. Offerors may wish to consult their own lawyers on the appropriate forum for bringing an offer protest.

raw_text:
Communicate results

You must communicate the results of the evaluation of offers received as part of the solicitation process. Results must be communicated to the client, the successful offeror and the unsuccessful offerors.

Successful offeror(s)

After the evaluation process is complete, notify the successful offeror(s) as soon as possible. The notification should clearly state:

confirmation of their successful offer

the terms of the contract award, including key conditions and next steps

any additional documents required to finalize the contract

Ensure that the successful offeror understands their obligations and the expected timelines for contract performance. Clear communication at this stage helps avoid misunderstandings and sets the foundation for effective contract management.

You should also inform the client of the successful offer and provide details to ensure the smooth implementation of the contract.

Unsuccessful offerors

You should notify unsuccessful offerors as soon as possible after contract award or the issuance of a standing offer or supply arrangement. It is essential to communicate this information promptly to maintain a transparent and fair procurement process.

Sample letters are provided as templates for structuring the notification to unsuccessful offerors in a respectful and professional manner.

Refer to available samples of regret letters .

Recourse

Upon request, you should provide general information to offerors about the avenues that may be available for recourse, including judicial and quasi-judicial bodies to which offer protests can be made, such as the following:

Canadian International Trade Tribunal (CITT): Whether or not the CITT has jurisdiction to conduct an inquiry regarding any particular procurement will depend on factors such as the government institution conducting the procurement, the estimated value of the procurement, and the nature of the goods, services, or construction services being procured. More information can be obtained by visiting Canadian International Trade Tribunal .

Office of the Procurement Ombudsman (OPO): If the value of the procurement is below the monetary thresholds established under the Canadian Free Trade Agreement (CFTA) the OPO may have jurisdiction to conduct a review. More information can be obtained by visiting The Office of the Procurement Ombud .

An action in a Provincial/Territorial Superior Court or in Federal Court.

There are strict deadlines for bringing offer protests, and the time periods vary depending on where the offer protest is brought. Offerors may wish to consult their own lawyers on the appropriate forum for bringing an offer protest.

Communicate results

You must communicate the results of the evaluation of offers received as part of the solicitation process. Results must be communicated to the client, the successful offeror and the unsuccessful offerors.

Successful offeror(s)

After the evaluation process is complete, notify the successful offeror(s) as soon as possible. The notification should clearly state:

confirmation of their successful offer

the terms of the contract award, including key conditions and next steps

any additional documents required to finalize the contract

Ensure that the successful offeror understands their obligations and the expected timelines for contract performance. Clear communication at this stage helps avoid misunderstandings and sets the foundation for effective contract management.

You should also inform the client of the successful offer and provide details to ensure the smooth implementation of the contract.

Unsuccessful offerors

You should notify unsuccessful offerors as soon as possible after contract award or the issuance of a standing offer or supply arrangement. It is essential to communicate this information promptly to maintain a transparent and fair procurement process.

Sample letters are provided as templates for structuring the notification to unsuccessful offerors in a respectful and professional manner.

Refer to available samples of regret letters .

Recourse

Upon request, you should provide general information to offerors about the avenues that may be available for recourse, including judicial and quasi-judicial bodies to which offer protests can be made, such as the following:

Canadian International Trade Tribunal (CITT): Whether or not the CITT has jurisdiction to conduct an inquiry regarding any particular procurement will depend on factors such as the government institution conducting the procurement, the estimated value of the procurement, and the nature of the goods, services, or construction services being procured. More information can be obtained by visiting Canadian International Trade Tribunal .

Office of the Procurement Ombudsman (OPO): If the value of the procurement is below the monetary thresholds established under the Canadian Free Trade Agreement (CFTA) the OPO may have jurisdiction to conduct a review. More information can be obtained by visiting The Office of the Procurement Ombud .

An action in a Provincial/Territorial Superior Court or in Federal Court.

There are strict deadlines for bringing offer protests, and the time periods vary depending on where the offer protest is brought. Offerors may wish to consult their own lawyers on the appropriate forum for bringing an offer protest.
